title: The vulnerable male brain: Men's spatial abilities are condition-dependent, sexually selected traits
comment: Accepted manuscript, 8 pages, 2 figures. Accepted for publication on 5 May 2026. To appear in Evolution and Human Behavior. The Version of Record is available at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2026.106901. This manuscript may not exactly replicate the final published version
summary: Traits that are exaggerated in one sex relative to the other sex might be more vulnerable to stressor exposure because the development and expression of these traits are costly. Sex differences in such traits should therefore be smaller in populations with high stressor exposure. We tested this prediction in humans by examining the magnitude of men's advantage in spatial cognition and women's advantage in emotion recognition across nations that varied in their level of development. As predicted, men's advantage in spatial cognition was larger in nations relatively buffered from stressors. However, in contrast to our prediction, women's advantage in emotion recognition was constant across nations, suggesting aspects of men's cognition might be particularly vulnerable to early or current conditions. The samples were biased toward higher income and healthier individuals for nations in which men's spatial cognition was compromised; thus, we are likely underestimating the effects of living conditions on men's spatial cognition. The results further our understanding of how social and environmental conditions can have sex-specific effects on human cognition.

AI.tldr: Sexually selected traits (like men's spatial cognition advantage) are more vulnerable to stress, shrinking in harsher environments, while women's emotion recognition advantage remains stable across varying national development levels.
AI.motivation: To test the hypothesis that exaggerated sex-typical traits are more costly and thus more vulnerable to stressor exposure, leading to smaller sex differences in populations with high stress.
AI.method: Cross-national comparison of men's advantage in spatial cognition and women's advantage in emotion recognition across nations differing in development/stressor levels.
AI.result: Men's spatial cognition advantage was larger in nations buffered from stressors and smaller in harsh conditions. Women's emotion recognition advantage remained constant across nations. Sample biases suggest underestimation of living condition effects on men's spatial cognition.
AI.conclusion: Social and environmental conditions have sex-specific effects on cognition; aspects of men's cognition (especially sexually selected traits) may be particularly vulnerable to early or current adverse conditions.

title: TadA-Bench: A Million-Variant Benchmark for Future-Round Discovery Toward Agentic Protein Engineering
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026). Data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/JinGao/TadABench-1M . Code: https://github.com/shiyegao/TadABench-1M
summary: AI for scientific discovery is entering an agentic era, where protein-engineering systems are expected to prioritize future wet-lab experiments rather than merely fit static measurements. We introduce TadA-Bench, a million-variant wet-lab replay benchmark from 31 TadA directed-evolution rounds for future-round discovery toward agentic protein engineering. TadA-Bench preserves the campaign chronology and defines a fixed-data replay task: given earlier experimental rounds, models rank variants that appear only in later rounds. It provides aligned DNA, RNA, and protein views, and uses Seq2Graph, a graph-based label-unification pipeline, to reconcile noisy enrichment measurements into consistent cross-round activity labels. Random-split controls show strong interpolation, but future-round ranking and finite-budget candidate selection are much weaker. Controlled analyses suggest that evolutionary coverage is more informative than local data density, positioning TadA-Bench as a reproducible wet-lab replay substrate for future-round discovery toward agentic protein engineering; the data and code are released on Hugging Face and GitHub.

AI.tldr: TadA-Bench is a million-variant wet-lab replay benchmark from 31 rounds of TadA directed evolution, designed to evaluate agentic protein-engineering systems on future-round discovery rather than static data fitting.
AI.motivation: Existing protein-engineering benchmarks focus on fitting static measurements, but real-world scientific discovery requires prioritizing future experiments in an agentic, sequential decision-making setting.
AI.method: TadA-Bench preserves chronological campaign data, defines a fixed-data replay task (rank variants from later rounds given earlier rounds), provides aligned DNA/RNA/protein views, and uses Seq2Graph—a graph-based label-unification pipeline—to reconcile noisy enrichment measurements into consistent cross-round labels.
AI.result: Random-split controls show strong interpolation, but future-round ranking and finite-budget candidate selection are much weaker. Evolutionary coverage is more informative than local data density.
AI.conclusion: TadA-Bench offers a reproducible wet-lab replay substrate for evaluating agentic protein-engineering methods focused on future-round discovery; data and code are publicly released.

title: DXA-Derived Skeletal Phenotypes and Hip Fracture Risk: A Backdoor-Adjusted Causal Analysis
comment: 35 pages; main manuscript includes 4 figures and 3 tables; supplementary material includes 13 figures and 3 tables
summary: Purpose: To compare dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA)-derived hip skeletal phenotypes in relation to hip fracture risk using prespecified confounder adjustment and to assess whether phenotypes ranked by their backdoor-adjusted average treatment effects (ATEs) improve risk stratification. Methods: We analyzed 21,098 UK Biobank participants with linked health records, hip DXA-derived skeletal measures, and prespecified covariates. Sixteen phenotypes spanning bone mineral content (BMC), bone mineral density (BMD), and T-score across hip-related regions were evaluated. Confounder selection was guided by a prespecified directed acyclic graph (DAG). Backdoor-adjusted ATEs were estimated on the absolute risk-difference scale per standard deviation (SD) increase. Effect heterogeneity was evaluated for total femur BMD, and downstream prediction was assessed using clinical variables combined with phenotypes ranked by ATE magnitude. Results: Among 21,098 participants, 115 had hip fractures. All 16 phenotypes showed negative backdoor-adjusted ATEs per SD increase. The largest ATEs were observed for total femur BMC and total femur BMD, each with a risk difference of -0.0047, corresponding to approximately 4.7 fewer hip fractures per 1,000 participants per SD higher phenotype value. Conditional effects of total femur BMD were stronger among older participants and those with lower BMI. In prediction, clinical variables plus the top 11 ATE-ranked phenotypes achieved higher AUC than FRAX with femoral neck BMD (0.842 vs. 0.709), with higher sensitivity (0.748 vs. 0.443) and similar specificity (0.793 vs. 0.777). Conclusion: DXA-derived hip skeletal phenotypes differed in their backdoor-adjusted ATEs. Phenotype-level causal evaluation may help identify informative DXA measures for risk stratification.

AI.tldr: Study of 21,098 UK Biobank participants found that DXA-derived hip skeletal phenotypes (especially total femur BMC and BMD) have significant negative causal effects on hip fracture risk, and using the top 11 ATE-ranked phenotypes improved risk prediction (AUC 0.842) compared to traditional FRAX with femoral neck BMD (AUC 0.709).
AI.motivation: To compare DXA-derived hip skeletal phenotypes in relation to hip fracture risk using prespecified confounder adjustment and assess whether phenotypes ranked by backdoor-adjusted ATEs improve risk stratification.
AI.method: Analyzed 21,098 UK Biobank participants; 16 DXA-derived phenotypes evaluated; confounder selection via prespecified DAG; backdoor-adjusted ATEs estimated; effect heterogeneity evaluated; prediction performance assessed.
AI.result: All 16 phenotypes showed negative ATEs; total femur BMC and BMD had strongest ATEs (-0.0047); conditional effects stronger in older and lower BMI participants; top 11 ATE-ranked phenotypes achieved AUC 0.842 vs FRAX 0.709.
AI.conclusion: DXA-derived hip skeletal phenotypes differ in their backdoor-adjusted ATEs, and phenotype-level causal evaluation may help identify informative DXA measures for risk stratification.

title: Enhancing Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction with Hierarchical Motif-based Multimodal Protein Embedding
summary: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are essential for many biological processes. However, existing PPI prediction approaches suffer from two major limitations: they overlook the hierarchical organization of proteins, particularly meso-scale motifs that critically regulate PPIs, and fail to effectively integrate sequence, structure, and function modalities. To address these limitations, we propose MMM-PPI, a Hierarchical Motif-based Multi-Modal protein Encoder for PPI Prediction that constructs PPI embeddings in a bottom-up multi-modal manner across three scales. At the micro-scale, we encode three modal residue features; at the meso-scale, a novel multimodal motif encoder aggregates residues into spatially-informed motif embeddings; at the macro-scale, a multimodal protein encoder integrates motifs into protein embeddings by jointly modeling motif importance and inter-modal correlations. The pre-trained encoder can be used off-the-shelf for large-scale PPI prediction. Extensive experiments on multiple PPI datasets show that MMM-PPI outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label PPI prediction models, particularly under challenging data partitions and limited data scenarios. Codes are in https://github.com/yzf-code/MMM-PPI.

AI.tldr: MMM-PPI is a hierarchical multi-modal protein encoder that integrates sequence, structure, and function across micro, meso, and macro scales for improved PPI prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing PPI prediction methods overlook hierarchical protein organization (especially meso-scale motifs) and fail to effectively integrate sequence, structure, and function modalities.
AI.method: A three-scale framework: (1) micro-scale encodes residue-level multimodal features; (2) meso-scale uses a multimodal motif encoder to aggregate residues into motif embeddings; (3) macro-scale integrates motifs into protein embeddings via joint modeling of motif importance and inter-modal correlations.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art multi-label PPI prediction models, especially under challenging data partitions and limited data scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical motif-aware multi-modal encoding significantly improves PPI prediction, and the pre-trained encoder is available off-the-shelf for large-scale use.

title: Using Machine Learning to Enhance Hyperparameter Optimization in Pandemic Modeling: Case study of COVID-19 Dynamics in Ghana
summary: In this study, five distinct COVID-19 models developed in different countries, each designed to reflect the prevailing epidemiological condition at the time of formulation, are examined. The models are reformulated while still maintaining their original structure, using their common transmissions from one compartment to the other. Modified Patankar-Runge-Kutta (MPRK) methods are then applied to approximate the solutions of the resulting system of nonlinear ordinary differential equations (ODEs) representing each model to produce unconditionally positive approximations and to preserve the conservative part of the ODEs. In particular, we incorporate the numerical solution into a cost function to improve the estimates for the non-autonomous model hyperparameters. In a first step we obtain piecewise constant parameters that fit real data. Later we perform a WENO reconstruction in a post-process to approximate the true time-dependent coefficients inside the ODEs. As a proof-of-concept, we apply our approach to improve the parameters of a paper concerned with modeling COVID-19 in Ghana, where we can make 5-day predictions within a 10% error range.

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates five COVID-19 models from different countries, applies Modified Patankar-Runge-Kutta (MPRK) methods for positive and conservative numerical solutions, uses piecewise constant parameter fitting with WENO reconstruction for time-dependent coefficients, and demonstrates 5-day predictions within 10% error for a Ghana COVID-19 model.
AI.motivation: To improve COVID-19 model parameter estimation by combining multiple model structures, positivity-preserving numerical methods, and post-processing reconstruction techniques for better short-term predictions.
AI.method: Five existing COVID-19 models are reformulated while preserving original structure. Modified Patankar-Runge-Kutta (MPRK) methods solve the ODE systems ensuring unconditional positivity and conservation. Piecewise constant parameters are fitted to data, then WENO reconstruction approximates time-dependent coefficients in a post-processing step.
AI.result: The approach enables 5-day predictions within 10% error range when applied to a COVID-19 model for Ghana.
AI.conclusion: The combination of MPRK methods with WENO-based post-processing reconstruction provides a viable framework for improving parameter estimation and prediction accuracy in epidemiological modeling, demonstrated on COVID-19 models including a Ghana case study.

title: Self-Regulation through Communication in Evolved Neural Agents
summary: Communication is typically understood as indication: signals that transfer information from sender to receiver. We present a minimal predator avoidance task in which pairs of evolved CTRNN agents use communication for robust survival, and in which agents hear their own vocalizations, as in natural systems. Across 112 perfect-fitness agents from over 2,000 evolutionary runs, three dominant strategies emerge (accounting for 81% of agents): safety calling (39%), where agents signal from safe cover; alarm indication (22%), where agents vocalize when a threat is present without relying on self-hearing; and self-regulatory calling (20%), where agents depend on hearing their own call to sustain escape behavior. Self-hearing dependency is common among agents that call during an active threat (47%), but rare among agents that call only after reaching safe cover (10%; p < 10^-4). This pattern is consistent with a difference in causal order: safety callers act then communicate, while self-regulatory callers communicate in order to act. Removing self-hearing selectively impairs self-regulatory callers (fitness 0.40) while safety callers remain functional (0.90; p < 10^-9). These results show that communication can evolve to serve the caller's own behavioral regulation, not just information transfer to others.

AI.tldr: This paper studies evolved neural network agents in a predator avoidance task and finds three communication strategies emerge: safety calling (39%), alarm indication (22%), and self-regulatory calling (20%). Self-hearing is crucial for self-regulatory callers but not safety callers, showing communication can serve behavioral self-regulation rather than just information transfer.
AI.motivation: To challenge the conventional view that communication is solely for information transfer by investigating whether communication can evolve to serve the sender's own behavioral regulation in a minimal predator avoidance task.
AI.method: Evolved CTRNN (Continuous-Time Recurrent Neural Network) agents in a minimal predator avoidance task, analyzing 112 perfect-fitness agents from over 2,000 evolutionary runs. Agents could vocalize, hear their own vocalizations, and needed to survive predator encounters.
AI.result: Three dominant strategies emerged: safety calling (39%), alarm indication (22%), and self-regulatory calling (20%). Self-hearing dependency was common among agents that call during active threat (47%) but rare among safety callers (10%; p<10^-4). Removing self-hearing impaired self-regulatory callers (fitness 0.40) while safety callers remained functional (0.90; p<10^-9).
AI.conclusion: Communication can evolve to serve the caller's own behavioral regulation (self-regulatory calling) rather than solely information transfer to others, demonstrating that self-hearing plays a causal role in sustaining escape behavior for some evolved strategies.

title: BEAST3D: Animal behavioral analysis and neural encoding from multi-view video via Gaussian splatting
summary: Multi-view video recordings are increasingly used to capture the 3D movements of animals in experimental settings, yet extracting rich 3D representations from these recordings remains challenging. Supervised pose estimation requires extensive manual annotation, while general-purpose 3D reconstruction models trained on generic scene datasets fail on the specialized imagery and sparse-view setting of laboratory experiments. We address these limitations with BEAST3D, a self-supervised pretraining framework that learns 3D visual representations from unlabeled, calibrated multi-view video. BEAST3D uses a vision transformer to predict 3D Gaussian splats that reconstruct held-out views through differentiable rendering, while simultaneously segmenting the animal from the background. BEAST3D reconstructs 3D structure with as few as four views by conditioning directly on known camera parameters--unlike general-purpose models, which must estimate camera geometry from dense overlapping viewpoints that are seldom available in lab settings. Through comprehensive evaluation across four species, we demonstrate that BEAST3D produces rich, viewpoint-invariant features that transfer effectively to three downstream tasks: novel view synthesis, which validates the quality of the learned 3D representations; multi-view pose estimation, which provides the sparse keypoint trajectories widely used in behavioral analysis; and neural encoding, which relates 3D behavioral features to simultaneously recorded neural activity. BEAST3D thus establishes a versatile framework for behavioral analysis that leverages 3D structure in modern multi-view laboratory recordings.

AI.tldr: BEAST3D is a self-supervised pretraining framework that learns 3D visual representations from unlabeled multi-view video of animals, using vision transformers and 3D Gaussian splats to reconstruct views, segment backgrounds, and enable downstream tasks like pose estimation and neural encoding with as few as four camera views.
AI.motivation: Multi-view video captures 3D animal movements, but supervised pose estimation needs heavy manual annotation, and general-purpose 3D models fail on specialized lab imagery and sparse-view settings.
AI.method: BEAST3D uses a vision transformer to predict 3D Gaussian splats from calibrated multi-view video, reconstructing held-out views via differentiable rendering while segmenting the animal from background, conditioning directly on known camera parameters.
AI.result: Across four species, BEAST3D produces rich viewpoint-invariant features that succeed on novel view synthesis, multi-view pose estimation, and neural encoding tasks, requiring as few as four views.
AI.conclusion: BEAST3D establishes a versatile self-supervised framework for behavioral analysis that effectively leverages 3D structure from modern multi-view laboratory recordings.

title: Evolution of cooperation in two-level Prisoner's Dilemma
summary: We consider continuous Prisoner's Dilemma played in spatial setting by group-structured populations. The population dynamics consists of individual-level birth and death and group-level fission and extinction events. Each individual plays games with all other individuals within their group, while groups play games against their nearest neighbours. Payoffs from individual-level games affect birth rates of individuals, and payoffs from group-level games affect group extinction and fission probabilities. We show that a certain level of cooperation is maintained due to specific between-group dynamics even though the within-group evolution by itself always results in a complete loss of cooperation. The spatial nature of games and resulting fissioning and extinction events is essential for the evolution of cooperation: without it cooperation is never maintained. Analyzing various scenarios of between-group fission and extinction events, we find that higher levels of cooperation evolve when the selection affecting fission and extinction events is local rather than global.

AI.tldr: This paper studies continuous Prisoner's Dilemma in spatial, group-structured populations and finds that cooperation can be maintained through between-group dynamics (fission and extinction) even when within-group evolution alone would eliminate it, with local selection favoring higher cooperation levels.
AI.motivation: To understand how cooperation can evolve and persist in structured populations despite within-group selection pressures that favor defection.
AI.method: The authors model continuous Prisoner's Dilemma in a spatial setting with group-structured populations, incorporating individual-level birth/death and group-level fission/extinction events. Individuals play games within their group, while groups play against nearest neighbors. Payoffs affect birth rates (individual level) and fission/extinction probabilities (group level).
AI.result: Cooperation is maintained due to between-group dynamics, even though within-group evolution alone leads to complete loss of cooperation. Spatial structure and resulting fission/extinction events are essential. Higher cooperation evolves when selection on fission/extinction is local rather than global.
AI.conclusion: Spatial group-structured dynamics with local between-group selection can sustain cooperation where within-group evolution cannot, highlighting the importance of multi-level selection and spatial structure.

title: Evolution as a Process of Causal Inference
summary:
Recently, the mapping of the replicator equation onto Bayes' theorem has been recognised, leading to an analogy between evolutionary dynamics and Bayesian learning. However, this analogy holds only for pure selection in infinite populations and breaks down when mutations -- a central mechanism of evolution -- are introduced.
  Here I propose that evolution by natural selection, at least for populations of haploid replicators in static environments, is best understood not as a learning process but as a process of causal inference. Each mutation event constitutes a natural experiment in which the parent serves as the control and the mutant offspring as the treated unit. Natural selection screens the causal effect of the mutation on fitness, retaining mutations with non-negative effects. I formalise this view within the Neyman-Rubin potential-outcomes framework. I first develop the general theory using a generic fitness outcome and show how the core identification assumptions in causal inference (Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption, Consistency, Unconfoundedness, Positivity) map onto evolutionary biology.
  Using the unnormalised quasispecies equation, I prove that the intergenerational change in mean fitness decomposes exactly into a selection term -- recovering Fisher's Fundamental Theorem -- plus a mutation term that corresponds to a fitness-weighted average of the cumulated effect of all mutations over all parental genotypes. I show that this decomposition extends, under suitable assumptions, to the generalised replicator-mutator equation and that the frequencies of populations of matched parents-offspring update in proportion to the average causal effect of mutations on fitness.

AI.tldr: This paper reframes evolution by natural selection as a process of causal inference (not Bayesian learning), where each mutation is a natural experiment and natural selection screens causal effects on fitness, formalized via the Neyman-Rubin potential-outcomes framework.
AI.motivation: The existing analogy between replicator dynamics and Bayesian learning holds only for pure selection in infinite populations without mutations. Since mutation is a central evolutionary mechanism, this analogy is insufficient, motivating a new framework.
AI.method: The author maps the Neyman-Rubin potential-outcomes framework (SUTVA, Consistency, Unconfoundedness, Positivity) onto evolutionary biology, treating each mutation as a natural experiment (parent=control, offspring=treated). The unnormalized quasispecies equation is used to decompose fitness change into selection and mutation terms.
AI.result: The intergenerational change in mean fitness decomposes exactly into a selection term (recovering Fisher's Fundamental Theorem) plus a mutation term corresponding to a fitness-weighted average of cumulated effects of mutations. This decomposition extends to the generalized replicator-mutator equation.
AI.conclusion: Evolution by natural selection in haploid replicators in static environments is best understood as causal inference rather than Bayesian learning. Population frequencies update proportionally to the average causal effect of mutations on fitness.

title: Short-Term Synaptic Plasticity Stabilizes Goal-Conditioned Dynamics in a PFC-Inspired Reservoir Model for Multistep Goal-Directed Action Planning
comment: 68 pages, 33 figures, 3 tables; includes supplementary material; submitted to Neural Networks
summary: The prefrontal cortex (PFC) maintains goal information for action planning, but how recurrent circuits preserve it in an action-usable form over behavioral timescales remains unclear. Here we ask whether short-term synaptic plasticity (STP) can stabilize goal information as action-usable, goal-conditioned dynamics. We incorporated STP into a PFC-inspired reservoir computing model with basal-ganglia-inspired temporal-difference readout learning, and evaluated paired models with and without STP across 100 independently generated networks in a multistep goal-directed action-selection task with delayed execution. Goal identity was highly decodable during the delay even without STP, so STP was not required to form a linearly readable goal representation. Under state noise, however, success without STP fell from 75.8% to 49.5%, whereas the model with STP remained essentially unchanged (91.8% without noise versus 89.2% under noise; paired Cohen's dz=1.31). Time-resolved decoding, state-space separability, and action-value-difference analyses showed that STP preserved goal information as action-relevant goal-conditioned dynamics available at later action opportunities. Gain-matched and STP-state perturbation controls argued against a simple fixed recurrent-scaling explanation and supported online, history-dependent synaptic modulation. Effective-connectivity analyses showed delay-period goal-specific patterning that increased toward the later part of the trial with STP, where it should be read as goal- and task-state-conditioned patterning; effective connectivity without STP was time-invariant. A grid search identified a facilitation-dominant range of STP time constants associated with high success rates. These results suggest that STP supports robust goal-conditioned dynamics through dynamic modulation of goal-dependent effective recurrent connectivity.

AI.tldr: This study shows that short-term synaptic plasticity (STP) stabilizes goal-conditioned dynamics in PFC-inspired reservoir computing models, making them robust to noise during delayed action selection, unlike models without STP which degrade significantly under noise.
AI.motivation: To understand how recurrent circuits in the prefrontal cortex preserve goal information in an action-usable form over behavioral timescales, specifically whether short-term synaptic plasticity can stabilize this information.
AI.method: Incorporated STP into a PFC-inspired reservoir computing model with basal-ganglia-inspired temporal-difference readout learning; evaluated paired models with/without STP across 100 independently generated networks in a multistep goal-directed action-selection task with delayed execution.
AI.result: Models with STP maintained high success rates under noise (89.2%) compared to those without (49.5%). STP preserved goal information as action-relevant dynamics, showed time-variant effective connectivity, and was most effective with facilitation-dominant time constants.
AI.conclusion: STP supports robust goal-conditioned dynamics through dynamic modulation of goal-dependent effective recurrent connectivity, providing a neural mechanism for maintaining action-usable goal information over delays.

title: Who Is in Mind Matters: Attachment Representations in Early Childhood Synchronize Child-Adult Interacting Brains
summary: Human attachment is distinguished by enduring internalized representations that shapes neurodevelopment and social-emotional functioning. However, as unobservable inner processes mixed with social cues and partner-specific factors, the neurocognitive mechanisms of these representations during real-time interaction remain unclear. Using a novel Remote Partner-Belief Manipulation paradigm in 40 child-mother-stranger trios, we experimentally isolated attachment representations in 3-4-year-olds by manipulating children's partner-belief during remote cooperation. The inner processes were captured from synchrony between partners' EEG, showing that children's mother-partner belief, regardless of the actual partner, significantly enhanced interbrain synchrony. This partner-belief modulation concentrated on children's P4 channel (overlaying the attachment-designated right temporoparietal junction), where synchrony strength correlated to attachment security and children's response acceleration due to mother-partner belief. These findings established attachment representations as an independent, endogenous driver of interbrain synchrony, potentially via children's heightened attention towards their attachment figure, implying the role of symbolic attachment activation when separation.

AI.tldr: Using a Remote Partner-Belief Manipulation paradigm with 40 child-mother-stranger trios, this study shows that 3-4-year-olds' belief that they are cooperating with their mother (vs. a stranger) enhances interbrain synchrony, regardless of the actual partner. This effect localized to the right temporoparietal junction (P4 channel) and correlated with attachment security, demonstrating that attachment representations independently drive neural coupling during real-time interaction.
AI.motivation: To isolate and understand the neurocognitive mechanisms of attachment representations during real-time social interaction, as these internalized processes are typically confounded with social cues and partner-specific factors in natural settings.
AI.method: A Remote Partner-Belief Manipulation paradigm was used with 40 child-mother-stranger trios, manipulating 3-4-year-olds' belief about who they were cooperating with remotely, while capturing interbrain synchrony via EEG.
AI.result: Children's mother-partner belief (believing they were interacting with mother) significantly enhanced interbrain synchrony regardless of the actual partner. This modulation was concentrated on the P4 channel (right temporoparietal junction), and synchrony strength correlated with attachment security and children's behavioral response acceleration.
AI.conclusion: Attachment representations act as an independent, endogenous driver of interbrain synchrony, potentially through heightened attention toward the attachment figure, suggesting a role for symbolic attachment activation during separation.

title: The Epi-LLM Framework: probing LLM behavioral priors through epidemiological agent-based models
comment: Submitted to American Journal of Epidemiology
summary: Human behaviour during epidemics affects infectious disease dynamics, but quantifying this remains deeply challenging. Here we introduce the Epi-LLM framework: a novel integration of agent-based modelling, real-life epigames, and large language models (LLMs) in which a synthetic society of agents reasons and adapts dynamically over an outbreak contact network. Comparing synthetic agent behaviour against a no-intervention SEIR baseline and human participant data from the AUIB epigame study, we find that LLM agents across four different architectures reduced peak active infections, with quarantine compliance peaking at 58-65% on day six of the 15-day simulation. A binomial generalised linear model showed that perceived health severity was the strongest predictor of quarantine behaviour ($β= 0.33, p = 0.002$), yielding a pseudo-$R^2$ of 0.055, comparable to the 0.072 observed in the human trial. LLM architecture is a key determinant of epidemic dynamics: low-variance architectures offer greater internal validity for testing behavioural rules, while high-variance models may better represent real-world decision-making. Geographic labels alone do not induce culturally differentiated behaviour; explicit attitudinal parameterisation is required. This proof-of-principle work lays the groundwork for deploying the Epi-LLM framework as a scalable, risk-free simulation environment for pandemic preparedness research.

AI.tldr: The Epi-LLM framework integrates agent-based modeling, epigames, and LLMs to simulate dynamic human behavior during epidemics. LLM agents reduced peak infections with quarantine compliance peaking at 58-65%. Perceived health severity was the strongest predictor of quarantine. LLM architecture affects epidemic dynamics, and explicit attitudinal parameterization is needed for culturally differentiated behavior.
AI.motivation: Human behavior during epidemics affects disease dynamics but is extremely difficult to quantify. Existing models often fail to capture dynamic, adaptive human decision-making in outbreak settings.
AI.method: The Epi-LLM framework integrates agent-based modeling, real-life epigames, and LLMs to create a synthetic society of agents that reason and adapt dynamically over an outbreak contact network. Four different LLM architectures were tested and compared against a no-intervention SEIR baseline and human participant data.
AI.result: LLM agents reduced peak active infections across architectures. Quarantine compliance peaked at 58-65% on day six. Perceived health severity was the strongest predictor (β=0.33, p=0.002, pseudo-R²=0.055), comparable to human trials (0.072). Low-variance architectures offer better internal validity; high-variance models better represent real-world decisions. Geographic labels alone don't induce cultural differentiation.
AI.conclusion: The Epi-LLM framework is a scalable, risk-free simulation environment for pandemic preparedness research, capable of modeling dynamic human behavior during epidemics.

title: Computing the final epidemic size distributions of a multi-type Galton--Watson process
summary: The Galton--Watson process (GWP) is a discrete-time branching process model that provides a powerful tool for analyzing epidemic data and estimating key epidemiological parameters such as the basic reproduction number. When used with surveillance-based cluster size data, the GWP can also elicit information about the extent of transmission heterogeneity, even when each transmission process is not directly observable. When cluster size distribution data are available, the parameters that govern the transmission can be statistically inferred by using the probability mass function that corresponds to the observed cluster size data. For multi-type GWPs, however, real-world applications remain limited, possibly because of the absence of conceptually and practically straightforward approaches for deriving the closed-form solution of the final size distribution. In the present study, we propose a framework for computing the final size distribution of multi-type GWPs, using a method for the choice of the Cauchy integral contour. We provide examples of how our framework can be applied to both simulated data and real-world data of Middle East respiratory syndrome, and discuss potential pitfalls surrounding the identifiability of parameters for statistical inference when using likelihoods that are not conditioned on extinction.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a computational framework using Cauchy integral contour methods to derive the final size distribution of multi-type Galton-Watson processes, enabling epidemiological parameter inference from cluster size data, demonstrated with simulations and MERS data.
AI.motivation: Multi-type GWPs have limited real-world applications due to the lack of straightforward approaches for deriving closed-form solutions of final size distributions, hindering their use in epidemiological inference.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework that uses a Cauchy integral contour method to compute the final size distribution of multi-type GWPs, enabling statistical inference from observed cluster size data.
AI.result: The framework is demonstrated on both simulated data and real-world Middle East respiratory syndrome (MERS) data, showing practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The approach makes multi-type GWPs more accessible for real-world epidemiological analysis, though identifiability issues remain when using likelihoods not conditioned on extinction.

title: Learning to See via Epiretinal Implant Stimulation in silico with Model-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: 18 pages, 6 figures. Published version: Biomed. Phys. Eng. Express 10, 025006 (2024)
summary: Objective: Diseases such as age-related macular degeneration and retinitis pigmentosa cause the degradation of the photoreceptor layer. One approach to restore vision is to electrically stimulate the surviving retinal ganglion cells with a microelectrode array such as epiretinal implants. Epiretinal implants are known to generate visible anisotropic shapes elongated along the axon fascicles of neighboring retinal ganglion cells. Recent work has demonstrated that to obtain isotropic pixel-like shapes, it is possible to map axon fascicles and avoid stimulating them by inactivating electrodes or lowering stimulation current levels. Avoiding axon fascicle stimulation aims to remove brushstroke-like shapes in favor of a more reduced set of pixel-like shapes. Approach: In this study, we propose the use of isotropic and anisotropic shapes to render intelligible images on the retina of a virtual patient in a reinforcement learning environment named rlretina. The environment formalizes the task as using brushstrokes in a stroke-based rendering task. Main Results: We train a deep reinforcement learning agent that learns to assemble isotropic and anisotropic shapes to form an image. We investigate which error-based or perception-based metrics is adequate to reward the agent. The agent is trained in a model-based data generation fashion using the psychophysically validated axon map model to render images as perceived by different virtual patients. We show that the agent can generate more intelligible images compared to the naive method in different virtual patients. Significance: This work shares a new way to address epiretinal stimulation that constitutes a first step towards improving visual acuity in artificially-restored vision using anisotropic phosphenes.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using deep reinforcement learning to assemble isotropic and anisotropic phosphene shapes (brushstrokes) to render intelligible images for epiretinal implant patients, showing improved image intelligibility over naive methods across different virtual patients.
AI.motivation: Epiretinal implants produce anisotropic (elongated) shapes along axon fascicles, degrading visual quality. Recent work can avoid this but at the cost of reducing the number of usable phosphenes. The authors propose instead to leverage both isotropic and anisotropic shapes as brushstrokes to render more intelligible images.
AI.method: A deep reinforcement learning agent trained in the rlretina environment learns to assemble isotropic and anisotropic shapes for stroke-based rendering. The agent is trained using model-based data generation with a psychophysically validated axon map model, and tested with different error-based and perception-based reward metrics.
AI.result: The deep RL agent generates more intelligible images compared to naive methods across different virtual patients, effectively using both shape types as brushstrokes.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a novel approach for epiretinal stimulation that takes advantage of anisotropic phosphenes rather than suppressing them, representing a first step toward improving visual acuity in artificially-restored vision.

title: Formalizing the Binding Problem
summary: Representations of the world, arguably, contain information about features (e.g. something is blue, something is a circle) but also information about which features are part of the same object (e.g. the circle is blue), which we call binding information. Any system with the ability to understand scenes with multiple objects must be able to solve the binding problem: it needs to know which features belong together. However, despite work showing that Vision Transformers (ViTs) know which patches belong together, it is not known whether current deep learning models learn to exhibit binding information, i.e., for features. We may believe that there is not much binding information, after all misattributing features to wrong objects is a common failure of ViT-based architectures, especially in scenes with objects sharing features. Here we formalize the binding problem with an information-theoretic approach, and introduce a probing method to measure binding information in model representations. We perform experiments on ViTs, measuring binding from different components of the architecture, such as the image summary token [CLS] or the spatial tokens. We use datasets with different binding challenges, such as feature sharing, occlusion, and natural features, while comparing the performance of several pre-trained ViTs. Overall, our research demonstrates binding as a key ingredient to strong visual recognition and reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes the binding problem (knowing which features belong to the same object) using information theory and proposes a probing method to measure binding information in Vision Transformers (ViTs). Experiments across various ViTs and challenging datasets show binding is a key ingredient for strong visual recognition.
AI.motivation: Despite Vision Transformers knowing which patches belong together, it's unclear if they learn binding information for features. Misattributing features to wrong objects is a common failure of ViTs, especially when objects share features.
AI.method: The authors formalize the binding problem via an information-theoretic approach and introduce a probing method to measure binding information in model representations. They experiment on ViTs, measuring binding from different architectural components ([CLS] token, spatial tokens) across datasets with challenges like feature sharing, occlusion, and natural features.
AI.result: Binding information varies across different ViT components and is challenged by feature sharing and occlusion. The results demonstrate that binding is a key ingredient to strong visual recognition and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Binding information is measurable and crucial for robust visual recognition; current ViTs exhibit varying degrees of binding, and improving binding could address common failure modes in multi-object scenes.
